title: Craftsman Style Chest of Drawers
description: Craftsman style Seven Drawer Chest. Built with red oak, this Chest of Drawers makes a beautiful addition to any bedroom. This simple yet elegant design will become a family heirloom.

materials.name: Kreg 1 1/4" Coarse and Fine Pocket Screws
Kreg 1 1/4" Kreg Fine Pocket Screws and 4 washers

steps.title: Introduction
steps.body: Please be sure to read through complete instructions before you begin any work on this project. Also keep in mind that plywood may be slightly thinner than its stated thickness. So some minor adjustments to plans may be required. Another good practice to use is, after the original carcass is assembled, cut to fit all additional pieces that are needed. While every effort was made to make the cut dimensions listed as accurate as possible, cutting to fit will adjust for any slight discrepancies in your assembly.

steps.title: Cut 3/4" Oak pieces
steps.body: Using the dimensions in the following diagram cut the pieces required for assembly. Please note that the 8" drawer front, 9" drawer front and the top will require glueing up. Two pieces will be used for each of the drawer fronts and three pieces for the top. It is a good idea to label the individual boards that will make up the drawer fronts and top. It is also a good idea to go through the boards and select the parts of the boards that best match color and grain for the glue ups. When edge glued, these individual pieces will make wide boards that are larger than the finished drawers and top. That is OK as we will eventually cut them to size. Use 100 - 150 grit sandpaper to smooth the parts.

steps.title: Edge glueing
steps.body: For the drawer fronts and top we will edge glue the boards labeled in step 2. If you are uncomfortable doing this I have attached one site with good information on the process. Please feel free to look at other sites. I normally use my Jet jointer to square and flatten the edges. Lay out the boards to provide the best looking grain. Use multiple clamps to bring the boards together. Make sure that the edges fit together well. Release the clamps and spread Titebond I wood glue along all of the edges to be joined. Avoid using excess glue. Realign the boards and clamp snugly. A picture of a typical clamp up follows.

steps.title: Cut 1/4" Oak plywood pieces
steps.body: Cut the chest side panels, back and drawer bottoms from the 1/4" oak plywood sheet. Use 100 - 150 grit sandpaper to smooth the parts.

steps.title: Cut 3/4" Poplar frame pieces
steps.body: Cut the chest frame pieces from the 3/4" Poplar. Use 100 - 150 grit sandpaper to smooth the parts.

steps.title: Cut 1/2" Poplar drawer box pieces
steps.body: Cut the drawer box sides, fronts and back pieces from the 1/2" poplar. Alternatively you can use 1/2" plywood. Keep in mind that plywood might be slightly thinner than its stated 1/2" thickness. If that is the case you will need to make slight adjustments to the measurements or the drawers may be a little too loose. Use 100 - 150 grit sandpaper to smooth the parts.

steps.title: Assemble the eight chest frames
steps.body: Use a Kreg pocket hole Jig (set for 3/4") to drill all of the pocket holes for the frame assemblies. The attached diagram shows the approximate location of pocket holes in the frame assemblies. Please note that the top and bottom frames have 3 extra pocket holes on each of their outside edges to attach the side rails. The middle six do not need these holes. Once all pocket holes are drilled, assemble the five frames using wood glue and 1 1/4" Kreg coarse pocket screws. Check square and flatness.

steps.title: Preparing the front stiles
steps.body: Mark the leg cuts on the two outer front stiles. Using a band saw or jig saw cut the legs. Keep in mind that you will be cutting a left and right side...so keep the best looking side out on both.

steps.title: Preparing the side rear stiles
steps.body: Use a Freud (99-036) Adjustable Tongue and Groove Bit set mounted in a Kreg router table to cut grooves. The groove cutter bit is adjusted to cut 1/4" X 3/8" grooves in the center of the inside edges of the rear stiles. Alternatively the grooves can be cut on a table saw. Now mark the legs on the side back stiles. Using a band saw or jig saw cut the legs. To attach the 1/4" plywood back a 1/4" deep X 3/8" wide rabbit needs to be cut on the back inside edge of both rear side stiles. This can be done on a table saw by doing either several passes using a standard blade or single pass with a dado blade. Please be careful doing the rabbits as there is a left and right side and the rabbits should face inside.

steps.title: Preparing the side front stiles
steps.body: Use a Freud (99-036) Adjustable Tongue and Groove Bit set mounted in a Kreg router table to cut grooves. The groove cutter bit is adjusted to cut 1/4" X 3/8" grooves in the center of the inside edges of the side front stiles. Alternatively the grooves can be cut on a table saw. Now mark the legs on the front side stiles. Using a band saw or jig saw cut the legs.

steps.title: Preparing the side middle stiles
steps.body: Use a Freud (99-036) Adjustable Tongue and Groove Bit set mounted in a Kreg router table to cut the tongues and grooves. The groove cutter bit is adjusted to cut 1/4" X 3/8" grooves in the center of both edges of the center stiles. Then use the tongue cutter bit adjusted to cut 1/4" X 3/8" tongues on both ends of the center stiles. Alternatively the grooves and tongues can be cut on a table saw.

steps.title: Preparing the side rails
steps.body: Use a Freud (99-036) Adjustable Tongue and Groove Bit set mounted in a Kreg router table to cut the tongues and grooves in the six rails. Use the groove cutter bit adjusted to cut 1/4" X 3/8" grooves in the center of the top and bottom side rails inside edges and both edges of the side middle rails. Then use the tongue cutter bit adjusted to cut 1/4" X 3/8" tongues on both ends of the six rails. Alternatively the grooves and tongues can be cut on a table saw.

steps.title: Assembling the sides
steps.body: Using a side rear stile, two side center stiles, a side front stile, a top, middle and bottom side rail and four side panels - assemble a side. Use wood glue on the ends of the rails, on the center stile tongues and along the edges of panels. Clean up any excess glue with a damp rag. Clamp snugly, check for square and readjust if needed.Repeat the process for the other side.

steps.title: Drawer guides
steps.body: Accurately measure and mark the drawer guide placement on the bottom frame and the six middle frames. The distance between the inside of the drawer guides should be 28". Using wood glue and 1 1/4" brad nails attach two guides to the top of the seven frames (total of 14 guides). The guides should be square and parallel on each frame.

steps.title: Assemble frames and sides
steps.body: Prepare for assembly by very accurately measuring and marking the distance of the lower edge of the frames from the bottom feet. The bottom frame should be flush with the bottom side rail and the top frame should be flush with the top of the side. I assemble on my table saw as it is flat and large enough to hold the chest. Place the front of the sides and the front of the frames down as they should be flush. If you can get an extra pair of hands they could be quite helpful here. Line up each frame with its marks and attach to the side with 1 1/4" Kreg fine pocket screws. I usually attach all frames to one side then repeat the process on the other. Keep checking that the frames are going in square and parallel.

steps.title: Add front stiles
steps.body: Attach the two outside front stiles with wood glue and 1 1/4" brad nails. The stiles should be flush with the side front stiles and the top of the sides. Please be sure to attach with the feet in the correct direction.

steps.title: Add back
steps.body: Use wood glue along the back side rabbits and on the back edges of the frames. Lay the 1/4" plywood (good side out) in place. It should be flush with the bottom of the bottom frame and the top of the top frame and fit inside the rabbits. Use 1" pin nails to tack in place.

steps.title: Preparing the drawer boxes
steps.body: Using the 1/2" poplar drawer box sets cut in step 6 and the 1/4" plywood drawer bottoms cut in step 4 prepare the drawer assemblies. Please note that my drawer sizes are approximate and will vary depending on how you construct the box corners. I use a Freud (99-240) Drawer lock bit on my Kreg router table to cut all of the corners. I then use my table saw to cut a 1/4" deep X 1/4 inch wide dado in all sides and fronts/backs. This dado should be 1/4 inch up from the bottom of each board.

steps.title: Assemble drawer boxes
steps.body: Assemble using matching sides, back, front and a bottom. Use wood glue at each corner joint and on the edges of the plywood bottom. You can either clamp until the drawers are dry or use pin nails in each corner. Check for square and adjust if needed. Repeat this process six more times until all drawer boxes are complete.

steps.title: Fitting the drawer fronts
steps.body: Cut the 8" and 9" drawer blanks to final dimensions. In the front of all drawer boxes drill 2 small pilot holes approx. 2 inches in from the edges and half way down from the top. Start two 1" flat headed self tapping wood screws on inside of each box. Place the bottom drawer box into the chest facing out. From the inside of the drawer box attach the 9" drawer front to the box with the two 1" screws. The bottom drawer front should be flush with the bottom frame and equally spaced left to right. I use dimes as spacers. Place the next drawer box in the dresser. Attach the 8" drawer front as described for the 9" drawer. Watch bottom as well as and left/right spacing. Repeat this process with remaining drawers.

steps.title: Add the top
steps.body: Cut glued up top to final dimensions (36" X 21 1/4 ") The front and side edges of the top can be rounded over using a roundover bit and router or you can ease the edges with sandpaper...Your choice. Drill four 1/4" holes in the top frame. Place the top in position on the dresser with back flat edge flush with the back of the dresser and the top spaced evenly left to right (about 1"). From the underside of the top frame use four 1 1/4" Kreg fine pocket screws with washers to attach the top. The screws should be drawn snug but not over tight. This should hold the top in place but allow for any expansion or contraction of the top.

steps.title: Small Drawer Front
steps.body: I like the look, so I made the top drawer appear to be two small drawers. This is accomplished by using a table saw to cut a one blade width X 1/8" deep groove (top to bottom) in the center of the top drawer. Use the mitre gauge to keep the drawer square to the saw blade as you cut. Use a black Sharpie permanent marker to color in the groove.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Now that the build is complete fill all nail holes and other imperfections with wood putty, let dry. Then use 100 - 220 sandpaper to do a once over on all surfaces in preparation for the finish. Clean well to remove all dust. (I use compressed air and clean dry rags). To prevent any smell in the drawer boxes use 2 or 3 coats of a water based sealant like Minwax Polycrylic Satin finish on them. As we live in an adobe style house, I chose to use Weathered Oak Minwax Stain. After the stain was dry I used Minwax Tung Oil finish. I gave the dresser three coats following manufacturers instructions. After the finish dries add the drawer pulls.
